summary: Jeff Atwood argues against relying solely on Akismet for blog spam protection, despite its effectiveness as a community-powered blacklist service. He explains the fundamental concepts of whitelists, blacklists, and greylists, noting that each approach has inherent weaknesses including false positives and whitelist attacks. The post emphasizes that depending on any single external service creates dangerous dependencies—if Akismet goes down, blogs are either flooded with spam or unable to accept comments. Atwood advocates for defense in depth, combining CAPTCHA to verify human commenters with Akismet as a secondary filter for human-entered spam.
key_points: Blacklists, whitelists, and greylists each have distinct weaknesses—blacklists generate false positives, whitelists can be exploited by spammers using legitimate URLs, and greylists still require human moderation time
Relying on a single external service creates unacceptable dependencies: downtime means either spam floods or disabled comments
Akismet struggles with edge cases like compliment spam, gibberish from spammers testing algorithms, and human-crafted contextual spam messages
Defense in depth is essential—CAPTCHA validates human presence while Akismet catches human-entered spam, addressing different attack vectors
The scale problem is real: with 75 spam trackbacks per hour on one blog, expecting any single service to handle the entire internet's spam is unreasonable
money_quotes: The core problem is relying on a single method of defense against spam.
I hate dependencies, and I always strive to keep the number of dependencies I accept to an absolute minimum.
If you consider Akismet a success because you ignore the moderation queue entirely, have you really succeeded?
Abandoning CAPTCHA in favor of Akismet is unforgivable.
For best results, use defense in depth and combine local anti-spam measures, such as CAPTCHA, with Akismet as a backup.
key_insight: Never outsource your entire defense against any threat to a single external service—true security requires layered, independent protection mechanisms you control.
